nativeRange.habitat: Rocky deepwater zones of Lake Tanganyika, typically found in the northern portion of the lake at depth over rocky/sandy substrates

decorPreferences: Large cave formations or overhanging rockwork
Open swimming areas between rock structures

diet.notes: Primarily accepts frozen and prepared meaty foods — krill, mysis shrimp, silversides, prawns, and earthworms are commonly recommended. High-quality sinking cichlid pellets are generally well-accepted. Feeder fish and mammalian/avian meat products are best avoided to reduce disease risk and inappropriate fat content.

goodWith: Other large Tanganyikan cichlids (Altolamprologus, Julidochromis, Lepidiolamprologus)
Larger Tropheus species (in sufficiently large tanks)
avoidWith: Small fish that fit in its mouth (predatory of small fish)
Mbuna-type cichlids (incompatible water chemistry preferences and aggression mismatch)

summary: An iconic deepwater cichlid from Lake Tanganyika, known for its distinctive nuchal hump and striking blue-and-white striped patterning. Long-lived and generally slow-moving; best kept as a harem group in a large, hard alkaline aquarium.
careNotes:
Cyphotilapia frontosa is endemic to Lake Tanganyika and requires hard, alkaline rift-lake water chemistry — untreated soft tap water is generally unsuitable without buffering. This species is noted as a deepwater fish in the wild (typically 15–70 m, with records down to 120 m), and tends to prefer lower light levels and a calm aquarium environment. A minimum group of 5 is broadly recommended, with 8–12 being closer to what larger sources suggest for stable social dynamics; the classic harem structure is one dominant male with multiple females.

Several geographic color variants are recognized in the trade: 'Burundi' (classic 6-stripe blue/white), 'Mpimbwe' (blue stripes on white/gray body, found in southern Tanzania), 'Zaire Blue' or 'Zaire' (more extensive blue coloration), and 'Kigoma' (broader dark bars). A specialized blue-fluorescent captive-bred strain has also emerged in the hobby. These variants are sometimes sold under their location names and command a price premium.

This species is predatory toward fish small enough to swallow — small tetras, danios, and juvenile cichlids are at risk in the same tank. It is NOT suitable for a general community setup. Its large adult size (commonly 30–35 cm in aquaria, with wild specimens reportedly reaching ~40 cm) and group requirements mean substantial tank footprint (180+ cm length, 500+ L) is needed for a proper harem.

Frontosa are notably long-lived — 15–25 years in well-maintained aquaria is commonly reported, making this a significant long-term commitment. Growth is slow; juveniles may take several years to develop the characteristic nuchal hump, which is more pronounced in males.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. The female broods eggs buccally for approximately 35–50 days (some sources cite up to 6 weeks). Clutch sizes are relatively small — typically 10–50 eggs — compared to many other cichlids. Females practice 'buccal feeding,' passing food to fry while still holding them. Breeding in captivity is considered moderately difficult; a well-conditioned harem group in a stable, mature tank is the typical approach. The male-to-female ratio of the group (1M:4–6F) and patience are key factors cited by keepers.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op, which has no dedicated Frontosa care page. SeriouslyFish provides the most comprehensive species-specific data for this taxon.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (30–35 cm), temperature (22–28°C), pH (8.0–9.0), tank size, and lifespan (15–25 years). Minor disagreement on minimum tank size (125 gal / ~473 L vs SeriouslyFish's 864 L for a group); biased toward the larger SeriouslyFish recommendation given the group-living and predatory size requirements. Breeding difficulty listed as 'easy' vs SeriouslyFish 'moderately challenging'; resolved to 'moderate' consistent with user-provided key data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: confirms Cichlidae, Cichliformes (modern classification). Max TL 33 cm consistent with captive range. IUCN Near Threatened status noted. Hardness range 8–12 dH on FishBase is narrower than hobby sources; hobby sources used for aquarium parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; buccal incubation 35–50 days; clutch 10–50 eggs. Requires established harem group and stable water for reliable spawning.
